claims.claim: The Prevention of Crime Act 1959, amended in 2014, lets an appointed Prevention Board impose 2-year detention orders renewable indefinitely.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297, Prevention of Crime Act 1959, online updated reprint as at 1 January 2018, MOHA PDF, sections 7B and 19A(1), https://moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf
claims.correction: More precise wording: the Board may detain a registered person for a period not exceeding two years and may renew the order for further periods not exceeding two years at a time. The Act does not state an express maximum number of renewals.
claims.stale_check: No contrary newer amendment found; the checked MOHA reprint is as at 1 January 2018 and remains the official text located.

claims.claim: The 2014 amendment removed judicial review of Board decisions.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297, section 15B, MOHA PDF; list of amendments says section 15A/15B was amended/inserted by Act A1459 effective 2 April 2014, https://moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf
claims.correction: Section 15B does not remove all judicial review. It bars review of discretionary acts or decisions except on questions of compliance with procedural requirements under the Act. It also defines judicial review to include habeas corpus, declarations, injunctions and prerogative orders.
claims.stale_check: Federal Court press summary in Rovin Joty Kodeeswaran v Lembaga Pencegahan Jenayah, dated 19 February 2021, states section 15B was upheld as constitutional by the majority. No later primary source found overturning that point.

claims.claim: Detainees enter via 60-day police investigation periods (24h initial + 21-day + 38-day extensions) before the Prevention Board hearing.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297, sections 3(2), 4(1) and 4(2), MOHA PDF, https://moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf
claims.correction: The sequence is accurate if expressed as up to 24 hours before production before a magistrate, followed by 21 days remand and then 38 days remand if statutory statements are produced. The statute says the person may be released sooner.

claims.claim: The Board's reasoning is not subject to High Court scrutiny.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297, section 15B, MOHA PDF; Federal Court press summary, Rovin Joty Kodeeswaran v Lembaga Pencegahan Jenayah, dated 19 February 2021, https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Ringkasan_Media/2021/Press%20Summary%20-%20Rovin%20Joty%20%28Majority%29%2019.2.2020_0.pdf
claims.correction: Better: the merits of the Board's discretionary decision are substantially insulated from review, but courts retain jurisdiction over procedural compliance and habeas corpus within the limits recognised by section 15B and case law.
claims.stale_check: The 2021 Federal Court majority upheld section 15B; no later primary reversal found in this check.

claims.claim: Board members are appointed by the Yang di-Pertuan Agong on the minister's advice.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297, section 7B, MOHA PDF; Federal Constitution Article 40 via WIPO Lex text, https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823
claims.correction: Section 7B states Board members are appointed by the Yang di-Pertuan Agong. It does not expressly state 'on the minister's advice'. That may be inferred from general constitutional advice rules, but the analysis should not present it as the wording of POCA unless citing Article 40 and explaining the inference.

claims.claim: There is no public reasoning standard.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297, sections 9, 10, 10A, 15B and 19A, MOHA PDF, https://moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf
claims.correction: The Act provides for Inquiry Officer reports and Board decisions but I did not find a specific public-reasons publication standard. The claim should be framed as 'I found no statutory requirement in the checked Act for published reasons', not as a categorical fact about all rules or practice.
claims.stale_check: No newer primary source on publication practice found.

claims.source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Article 5, WIPO Lex text, https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823
claims.correction: Accurate, but a fuller statement should note Article 5 also includes habeas corpus protections for unlawful detention.

claims.claim: POCA operates as a constitutional carve-out under Article 149.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297 preamble, MOHA PDF; Federal Constitution Article 149, WIPO Lex text, https://moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf and https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823
claims.correction: Accurate in substance: POCA expressly states it is enacted pursuant to Article 149, and Article 149 validates qualifying laws notwithstanding inconsistency with Articles 5, 9, 10 or 13.

claims.claim: Article 149 specifically enables Parliament to enact preventive-detention laws notwithstanding other liberty protections.
claims.source: Federal Constitution Article 149 and Article 151, WIPO Lex text, https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823
claims.correction: Article 149 validates special laws designed to stop or prevent specified threats notwithstanding inconsistency with Articles 5, 9, 10 or 13. Preventive detention safeguards are separately addressed in Article 151. The claim is directionally right but compressed.

claims.claim: Civil-society reports indicate POCA detainees are disproportionately drawn from lower-income communities, though disaggregated official data is not published.
claims.source: SUHAKAM Annual Report 2017 search result indicated complaints that POCA detainees were mostly from poor families, but full primary verification of the exact lower-income/disproportionate claim was not completed from a primary source in this check: https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/2017_Annual-Report.pdf
claims.correction: Do not state 'disproportionately' unless backed by denominator-based socioeconomic data. Safer wording: SUHAKAM recorded complaints that some POCA detainees were from poor families; I did not verify official income-disaggregated POCA detainee data.
claims.stale_check: Potentially stale: government disclosed some demographic breakdowns for 2018-2022 through Home Ministry answers reported in December 2024, but I did not locate primary income-disaggregated data.

claims.claim: The asymmetry between civil-society reporting and official disclosure is itself a structural transparency gap.
claims.source: No primary source found establishing the broader evaluative claim; partial context from MOHA/Parliament-reported data appears in The Star report dated 12 December 2024, but primary parliamentary text was not located.
claims.correction: This is an interpretive claim. It should be supported by a specific audit of what official POCA data is and is not published, ideally citing parliamentary answers or MOHA datasets.
claims.stale_check: Possible stale issue because the Home Ministry reportedly disclosed 2018-2022 demographic data in December 2024.

claims.claim: POCA is not an emergency power that has been reluctantly retained. It is an active, routine detention regime, just one without a courtroom.
claims.source: POCA is an Act enacted pursuant to Article 149, not Article 150 emergency powers: Act 297 preamble and Federal Constitution Articles 149-150. Bernama reported 10,012 actions under POCA from 2 April 2014 to 10 March 2022: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2061460
claims.correction: Accurate that POCA is not an Article 150 emergency ordinance and has been used operationally, but 'active, routine detention regime' needs a date-qualified source. Available verified data supports substantial use through March 2022; current detention-order use in 2026 was not verified from a primary source.
claims.stale_check: Important: later reporting says POCA enforcement was suspended in 2022 after a Federal Court ruling, so the phrase 'active, routine' may be stale or misleading if read as current.

claims.claim: Like a prison sentence decided by an appointed committee: same outcome, no courtroom, no published reasoning.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297, sections 7B, 10A, 15B and 19A, MOHA PDF, https://moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf
claims.correction: This analogy overstates equivalence to a prison sentence. POCA detention is administrative preventive detention of registered persons, not a criminal conviction or sentence, though it can result in detention for up to two years renewable. It should preserve that legal distinction.
claims.stale_check: No newer primary source found changing the statutory distinction.

claims.claim: Preventive detention achieves imprisonment without the constitutional safeguards of a criminal trial.
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 297, sections 9, 10A, 15B and 19A; Federal Constitution Articles 149 and 151, https://moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf and https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823
claims.correction: The core point is supported: POCA allows detention without criminal trial. But it is incomplete because Article 151 provides preventive-detention safeguards, including grounds, representation and advisory board consideration, subject to national-interest limits.

claims.claim: POCA reform requires either restoring judicial review of Board decisions or publishing disaggregated outcome data; preferably both.
claims.source: Normative recommendation; no primary source can verify the word 'requires'. Relevant legal source: Act 297 section 15B, MOHA PDF, https://moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf
claims.correction: Frame as an editorial recommendation, not a factual requirement. If kept, source it to named reform proposals by SUHAKAM, Bar Council, SUARAM, or government consultation documents.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: POCA detention orders run 2 years and renew indefinitely, and the 2014 amendment removed judicial review of the appointed Board's decisions.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The renewable two-year point is supported, but 'removed judicial review' overstates section 15B because the Act preserves review for procedural compliance and the Federal Court majority described section 15B as maintaining judicial power with the courts.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Act 297 section 15B, MOHA PDF; Federal Court press summary in Rovin Joty Kodeeswaran v Lembaga Pencegahan Jenayah, dated 19 February 2021.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Board members are appointed by the Yang di-Pertuan Agong on the minister's advice.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: POCA section 7B verifies appointment by the Yang di-Pertuan Agong but does not expressly say 'on the minister's advice'. That requires separate constitutional inference under Article 40 and should be identified as such.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Civil-society reports indicate POCA detainees are disproportionately drawn from lower-income communities, though disaggregated official data is not published.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: I found indications of complaints about poor families, but no primary denominator-based data verifying disproportionate socioeconomic distribution, and no comprehensive primary audit proving non-publication of all relevant official disaggregated data.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: SUHAKAM Annual Report 2017 was indicated by search result but exact claim not fully verified from the report text in this check.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: POCA is not an emergency power that has been reluctantly retained. It is an active, routine detention regime, just one without a courtroom.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: POCA is not an Article 150 emergency power and has been used extensively, but 'active, routine' is not date-qualified and may mislead if enforcement has been suspended since 2022. It should distinguish statutory availability, historical use, and current enforcement.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Act 297 preamble; Federal Constitution Articles 149 and 150; Bernama report dated 13 March 2022 citing POCA Board member Khalil Azlan Chik.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Like a prison sentence decided by an appointed committee: same outcome, no courtroom, no published reasoning.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The analogy blurs preventive detention with a criminal prison sentence. POCA detention may result in confinement, but legally it is administrative preventive detention of registered persons, not punishment following conviction.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The original analysis omits Article 151 safeguards while saying POCA lacks criminal-trial safeguards.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Article 151 specifically provides preventive-detention safeguards: grounds, opportunity to make representations, and advisory board review for citizens, with national-interest nondisclosure limits. Omitting this makes the constitutional framework appear simpler than it is.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Constitution Article 151, WIPO Lex text.

omitted_facts: POCA section 19A(4) entitles a person served with a detention order to make representations to an Advisory Board constituted under Article 151(2) of the Federal Constitution, according to the MOHA Act 297 PDF.

Federal Constitution Article 151 provides preventive-detention safeguards, including information on grounds, opportunity to make representations, advisory board consideration within three months for citizens, and a national-interest nondisclosure exception.

The Federal Court majority in Rovin Joty Kodeeswaran v Lembaga Pencegahan Jenayah upheld section 15B as constitutional and dismissed the appeals, according to the Judiciary press summary dated 19 February 2021.

A POCA Board member was reported by Bernama on 13 March 2022 as saying 10,012 people had been acted against under POCA from 2 April 2014 to 10 March 2022, including 2,673 detention orders, 6,537 supervision orders and 802 released.
POCA section 4(2A) states that no person shall be arrested and detained under section 4 solely for political belief or political activity.
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only 'T4A related issue 1988', which is circular and not a primary source. It does not cite the Act, the Federal Constitution, Federal Court cases, parliamentary answers, SUHAKAM reports, MOHA materials, Bar Council/SUARAM positions, detainee/community voices, or government security rationale. Source diversity is therefore extremely weak: one internal source, no identifiable political spectrum, no primary legal text, and no official or rights-body data in the published card source list.
overall_assessment: The legal core is partly sound: POCA permits renewable two-year detention orders by the Prevention of Crime Board, and it is enacted under Article 149. However, the analysis repeatedly over-compresses judicial review and constitutional safeguards. Section 15B restricts review but does not literally remove all judicial review, and Article 151 safeguards are omitted. The social-impact claim about lower-income detainees is not adequately verified. Reliability is moderate for broad legal direction, weak for precision and current framing.
